You are exactly right! This highlights the core architectural difference between the two tools. Webpack is a monolithic bundler that contains all its own build machinery, whereas Vite is a dev server that delegates its underlying compilation to esbuild.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Webpack vs Vite for Angular appsPress enter or click to view image in full size

Webpack vs Vite in development workflows💡 If you’re starting fresh today — use Vite. If you’re maintaining an existing project — stay on Webpack unless you have time to migrate.
💡 Why esbuild? esbuild is written in Go and runs natively on your machine. It is 10–100x faster than JavaScript-based tools like Babel.
The fundamental difference lies in how they process your code for local development:
1. Webpack (Bundle-First)

• Architecture: Webpack handles everything, including bundling, transpilation, and asset loading, right out of the box using a plugin-based architecture (e.g., loaders for CSS, TS, Babel).

• Workflow: Before your development server can start, Webpack must crawl your entire dependency graph and bundle your whole application into static files.

• Trade-off: This results in slow initial startup times and heavy CPU usage, especially as project size grows.
2. Vite (Native ESM + esbuild)

• Architecture: Vite is a lightweight dev server that does not bundle your source code before serving it.

• Workflow: It serves your source code over Native ES Modules (ESM), allowing the browser itself to act as the bundler.

• The Role of esbuild: Vite relies on esbuild (written in Go) to instantly pre-bundle heavy third-party dependencies (like React or Lodash) into ESM and transpile your TS/JSX on-demand.

• Trade-off: Because it offloads this step, dev server start-ups take mere milliseconds
Vite Dev Mode — Step by Step
Step 1 — Start the Development Server
npm run devVite starts its development server almost instantly.
Why is it so fast?

• It does not bundle your application upfront.

• It starts an HTTP server and a WebSocket server.

• It scans dependencies once (called dependency pre-bundling) using esbuild.

• Your application source code (src/) is not bundled at startup.
npm run dev
│
▼
Start HTTP Server
│
▼
Start WebSocket Server
│
▼
Pre-bundle dependencies
(node_modules only)
│
▼
Ready in a few hundred millisecondsCorrection: Saying “Vite has nothing to bundle” is slightly inaccurate. Vite does pre-bundle dependencies from node_modules with esbuild to improve performance, but it does not bundle your application source code during development.
Step 2 — Browser Loads the Application
Vite serves index.html directly.
<script type="module" src="/src/main.js"></script>The browser sees:
type="module"and uses the browser’s native ES Module loader.
Browser
│
▼
Request index.html
│
▼
Request /src/main.js
│
▼
main.js imports App.js
│
▼
Browser requests App.jsUnlike Webpack, Vite lets the browser resolve the module graph.
Step 3 — On-Demand Transformation
Suppose the browser requests:
/src/App.jsVite:

• Reads the source file.

• Applies plugins (if needed).

• Uses esbuild for fast transformations (TypeScript, JSX, etc.).

• Returns standard JavaScript.

• Caches the transformed result.
Browser requests App.js
│
▼
Read file
│
▼
Transform
(JSX → JS
TS → JS)
│
▼
Return JavaScript
│
▼
Cache resultOnly requested files are transformed.
Example:
500 ComponentsHome Page
│
├── Header
├── Footer
└── Dashboard
If you never visit the Dashboard page:
Dashboard.jsx↓
Never transformed
This lazy transformation is one of Vite’s biggest performance advantages.
Step 4 — HMR (Hot Module Replacement)
When you save a file:
App.jsVite:

• Detects the file change.

• Invalidates the cached transformed module.

• Re-transforms only that module.

• Sends an HMR update message via WebSocket.

• The browser requests the updated module.

• The HMR runtime swaps the module.

• The framework (Angular, React, Vue, etc.) applies the update if possible.
Save App.js
│
▼
File watcher detects change
│
▼
Invalidate cache
│
▼
Transform changed file
│
▼
WebSocket notification
│
▼
Browser requests updated module
│
▼
HMR Runtime
│
▼
Framework Runtime
│
▼
Update UIVite only invalidates and updates the affected module and the minimal set of modules that depend on it. It does not rebuild the entire application bundle.
Webpack vs Vite
| Feature | Webpack | Vite |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Development startup** | ❌ Slower (creates an application bundle before serving) | ✅ Very fast (serves source files as native ES modules; only pre-bundles dependencies) |
| **Incremental rebuild/HMR** | Good, but rebuilds affected bundles and traverses more of the module graph | Excellent, transforms only affected modules and propagates updates to HMR boundaries |
| **Development bundling** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (application source isn't bundled during dev) |
| **Production bundler** | Webpack | Rollup (default) |
| **Production transformation** | Babel / loaders / plugins | esbuild (transforms & minification by default) + Rollup |
| **Configuration** | More flexible, but verbose | Simpler with sensible defaults |
| **Plugin ecosystem** | Very mature and extensive | Large and growing rapidly |
| **Tree shaking** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (via Rollup) |
| **Code splitting** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Lazy loading** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **TypeScript support** | Via loaders (for example `ts-loader` or Babel) | Built-in transpilation using esbuild (type checking is typically done separately with `tsc`) |
| **JSX support** | Via Babel/loader configuration | Built-in |
| **CSS Modules** | Via loaders | Built-in |
| **Asset handling** | Via loaders/plugins | Built-in |
| **CommonJS support** | Native | Supported through dependency pre-bundling and plugins |
| **ES Modules** | Supported | Native-first design |
| **Legacy browser support** | Excellent (can target very old browsers with configuration) | Modern browsers by default; legacy support available via plugins |
| **Large enterprise projects** | Very common | Increasingly common for new projects |
| **Learning curve** | Steeper | Easier |
| **Angular support** | Angular 16 and earlier (default builder) | Angular 17+ (default development builder) |Production Bundle Size
Your statement is essentially correct:
Both Webpack and Vite can produce highly optimized production bundles. The biggest difference is the development experience rather than production performance.
A small refinement is that bundle size is determined more by your application’s code, dependencies, and build configuration than by the choice of bundler. Both Webpack and Rollup (used by Vite for production) support:

• ✅ Tree shaking

• ✅ Dead code elimination

• ✅ Code splitting

• ✅ Dynamic imports

• ✅ Asset optimization

• ✅ Long-term caching
For many applications, the resulting production bundle sizes are very similar. Rollup may sometimes generate slightly smaller bundles for library-style projects because of its module-centric optimization, while Webpack’s optimizations are also highly effective for large applications.
Why Vite is faster than Webpack ?
Vite is a modern build tool that uses native ES Modules in development instead of bundling. Unlike Webpack, which bundles your entire app before starting the dev server, Vite starts instantly and transforms files on-demand when the browser requests them. It uses esbuild (written in Go) for fast JSX transformations and pre-bundles only node_modules. In production, it uses Rollup for optimized output.
Summary

• Webpack: Built-in HMR using module.hot; works well with vanilla JavaScript and many frameworks.

• Vite: Built-in HMR using import.meta.hot; the preferred modern approach for vanilla JavaScript and frameworks.

• esbuild: Extremely fast compiler/bundler, but does not include a built-in HMR runtime. HMR must be supplied by a development server or framework layered on top of esbuild.

• Webpack excels in flexibility, mature ecosystem, and compatibility with complex or legacy projects.

• Vite focuses on an outstanding developer experience through native ES modules, on-demand transformation, and fast HMR.

• Production performance is usually comparable. The primary advantage of Vite is much faster development startup and incremental updates, not dramatically smaller production bundles.
